text: Robotic gastrectomy due to chronic gastro pleural fistula secondary to leak from sleeve gastrectomy. We present a case of a twenty four year old female whose eleven months post a sleeve gastrectomy, complicated by a leak. Initially treated with TPN and stent placement. She now presents with fever and cough and has a recurrent leak into the left chest, pleural cavity, and into the left lung. Plans were made for esophagogastrectomy using the robotic approach to deal with this chronic fistula.

The surgeon separates the stomach from the left lobe of the liver, identifying the right crus of the diaphragm. The sleeve is approached posteriorly and separated from inflammatory tissue near the angle of His and the left hemidiaphragm. The inflamed left hemidiaphragm and inflammatory omentum are visible, along with the fistula identified using a suction tip. The opening at the sleeve esophageal junction is noted. Transection begins with the left gastric vessels being taken using a linear stapler, and the hiatus is freed as the esophagus is dissected.

The surgeon continues the dissection posterior to the sleeve at the gastroesophageal junction, moving towards the left side near the left hemidiaphragm and fistula. The sleeve is rotated medially, and the fistula is completely transected from the left hemidiaphragm using robotic technology, which provides enhanced three-dimensional visualization.

The surgeon identifies the left crus and opens the hiatus on the left side, freeing and mobilizing the esophagus intra-abdominally. Both right and left cruci are visible. The sleeve is transected in its mid portion using a linear stapler, and an opening is created in the proximal sleeve near the gastroesophageal junction for the passage of the anvil into the esophagus.

The surgeon passes the anvil into the distal esophagus, preparing for esophageal transection. The distal esophagus is transected, avoiding the anvil, and the specimen is removed for pathological examination. The anvil is delivered through the distal esophagus, and the small bowel is prepared for the esophago-jejunostomy anastomosis using a circular stapler.

The surgeon connects and closes the anvil and circular stapler to perform the esophago-jejunal anastomosis. The circular stapler is then removed, and the staple line is reinforced with interrupted sutures. An endoscopy is conducted to assess the integrity of the anastomosis and confirm the anatomical configuration.
The surgeon clamps the anastomosis and insufflates it with air while submerged underwater, observing for any leakage of bubbles, which indicates the integrity of the anastomosis.
